detail:
Coverage BREADTH is strong and adequate to support the flat-curve headline; coverage DEPTH for per-card verdicts (especially boss-level) is thin and confounded, so card-level conclusions should not be trusted.

BREADTH (good): All 164 cards are simulated. Every card appears in >=4 decks (median 21, min 4), in >=17 matchups (median 84), across multiple loop bands (every card sees loop multipliers spanning 1.0->1.6), and every card faces at least one boss. The game has only two hero classes (types.ts line 260: "warrior" | "mage" | "neutral"), and the 443-deck set covers both (warrior 199, mage 244); cards carry no classRestriction in cards.json (all "none"), so the two classes legitimately exercise the full pool. Critically for the headline, all 164 cards appear in opt-OR-mixed ("good build") decks, so the "optimized comfortable" leg is not breadth-starved.

DEPTH (concern): (1) 72/164 cards (53 of them T3) NEVER appear in an "optimized" deck. The headline's "optimized" column is therefore a curated 92-card subset (and as few as 13 decks / 13 boss matchups per band), not a representative sample of the pool. The flat-curve claim survives only because "mixed" decks backfill the 164-card coverage. (2) Per-card verdicts in card-appearance.json are ATTRIBUTION-based, not isolation-based: analyze-validation-results.mjs (lines 186-190) credits the WHOLE deck's win/cushion to every card in it, with no swap/control. Boss verdicts are thin: 5 cards rest on <=4 boss matchups, 21 on <=6, 37 on <=8, at only 4 repeats each (per-matchup boss winRate is binary 0/1). So a card showing meanBossWinRate=1.0 may simply have ridden alongside strong cards.

VERIFICATION: I built a probe deck of the 5 thinnest-covered T3 cards (t3-counter-counter-earth, t3-counter-defense-earth, t3-air-attack-earth, t3-attack-attack-water, t3-defense-earth-fire) and simmed it at boss5/boss7. It CLEARED every normal deep enemy at full cushion (werewolf/depths_horror/skeleton 1.00 win, cushion 1.0) -- reinforcing the flat curve on normals -- but DIED 8/8 to boss_iron_golem and desert_golem and 4/4 to baby_dragon (mage). Yet card-appearance.json marks these same cards boss=1.0. This proves the per-card boss verdicts are noisy artifacts of co-occurring strong cards, not isolated card quality.
evidence: card-appearance.json: all 164 covered, nDecks min 4 / median 21 / max 136. From decks-v2-full.json: 72 cards never in an "optimized" deck (by tier: T1=2, T2=17, T3=53); deck quality dist random 120 / naive 71 / optimized 71 / mixed 181; distinct cards in optimized only 92/164, in mixed 164/164. quality-matrix.json optimized cells: band1-10 n=19, all other bands exactly 13 decks/13 boss matchups. boss7-optimized cell = 13 decks, classes {warrior:5,mage:8}, 91 distinct cards. results-v2.json: boss matchups use repeats=4, normal repeats=2, distinct boss winRates only {0,1}. Per-card boss-matchup counts: min 3, p25 9, median 16; <=4 boss matchups: 5 cards, <=6: 21 cards. Thinnest: t2-air-counter (nBoss=3), t3-attack-attack-water (nBoss=4). analyze-validation-results.mjs lines 186-190 attribute whole-deck winRate to every card. types.ts:260 classRestriction = warrior|mage|neutral (only two classes). PROBE SIM (5 thinnest T3 cards): boss7 vs werewolf wr=1 cush=0.59-1.0, vs depths_horror wr=1 cush=1.0, but vs BOSS_boss_iron_golem wr=0 deaths=8, baby_dragon(mage) wr=0 deaths=4; boss5 vs BOSS_desert_golem wr=0 deaths=8 -- all while card-appearance lists those cards boss=1.0.
impact_if_wrong: If card-level verdicts from this data are taken at face value, balance decisions will be made on noise: ~37 cards (notably thin T3) have boss "PASS" verdicts resting on <=8 single-deck attributions, and a card that actually fails bosses in isolation (proven above) is recorded as boss=1.0. Buffs/nerfs driven by card-appearance.json could move the wrong cards. For the FLAT-CURVE headline specifically, the impact is neutral-to-confirming: breadth is sufficient and my probe reproduced the flat curve on normals; the only place coverage HIDES difficulty is bosses, where weak/thin decks genuinely die (8/8) -- meaning the headline's "even random clears the final boss 0.90 win" may be optimistic for narrowly-built decks, but this is a thin-DEPTH boss-sampling issue, not a card-breadth gap that fabricates the flat curve.
recommended_action: 1) Do NOT publish per-card boss verdicts from card-appearance.json as-is; gate any card-level conclusion on a minimum sample (e.g. >=10 boss matchups across >=3 distinct decks) and flag the ~37 cards below that bar as "insufficient data." 2) For per-card causality, use isolation/swap testing (scripts/swap-test.mjs) rather than whole-deck attribution before claiming a card is fine or broken at bosses. 3) Add a small batch of "optimized" decks that intentionally feature the 72 never-optimized cards (mostly T3) so the optimized tier is a representative pool, not a curated subset. 4) Raise boss repeats above 4 (binary 0/1 per matchup gives ~+/-0.25 granularity) for any matchup feeding a per-card verdict. 5) Keep the flat-curve headline, but re-run the deep-boss leg with deliberately weak/thin decks (as in my probe) to confirm whether bosses are the one real difficulty knob the current sampling under-weights.

detail:
Card-coverage BREADTH is strong and supports the flat-curve headline; coverage DEPTH per quality-tier is lopsided enough to undermine PER-CARD balance verdicts (a separate question), but not the aggregate curve conclusion.

BREADTH (solid): All 164 cards are simulated (0 uncovered). Per-card median = 21 decks / 70 normal matchups; min = 4 decks / 13 matchups. Every card sees >=3 boss matchups in >=2 distinct boss decks, appears in >=3 distinct stages, and is tested in late content (boss3-7/loop25). Only 15/164 miss the deepest stage (boss7). 0 sim errors, 16 timeouts in 2045 matchups, no deck <4 distinct cards.

DEPTH GAP (concern): Quality-tier coverage is uneven. 72/164 cards (44%) NEVER appear in an optimized deck; 76/164 never in a naive deck; 45/164 never in ANY curated (naive OR optimized) deck — they live only in random/mixed decks. The analyzer's covered=true flag is a weak bar (>=1 deck, >=1 matchup), so per-card verdicts for ~45 cards (e.g. t3-counter-defense-water at 4 decks, t3-attack-attack-water at 5) rest on uncurated noise where the card may be incidental. Card-level balance claims for those should NOT be trusted in isolation.

WHY THE HEADLINE IS SAFE: The flat-curve claim is an AGGREGATE across quality tiers. Each band x quality BOSS cell pools 59-130 distinct cards — no thin card carries a band. Optimized still covers 92/164 cards across 15 well-formed archetypes (5 decks each). I built dedicated decks around the 4 thinnest cards at boss7 and they won WR=1.00 with cushion 0.43-0.66, matching the matrix's optimized deep-band figures — under-sampled cards reveal NO hidden mastery gradient. Lopsided coverage, if anything, biases optimized DOWNWARD (fewer reuses of top cards) yet it still clears, reinforcing the flat curve.
evidence:
Direct computation over tests/audit/val/decks-v2-full.json (443 decks: random 120, naive 71, optimized 71, mixed 181) and results-v2.json (2045 matchups):
- 164/164 cards covered; nDecks min/median/max = 4/21/136; nNormalMatchups min/median/max = 13/70/528 (card-appearance.json).
- cards in 0 optimized decks: 72; 0 naive: 76; never curated (no naive AND no optimized): 45. List incl. t3-counter-defense-water (4 decks), t3-attack-attack-water (5), t3-counter-defense-earth, t3-air-counter-counter, etc.
- per-card boss matchups min/median/max = 3/16/102; 0 cards with zero boss matchups; 0 cards in <2 distinct boss decks.
- distinct stages per card min/median/max = 3/8/12; 0 cards in <=1 stage; 0 cards absent from late content; 15/164 absent from boss7.
- boss matchup repeats=4, normal repeats=2; 0 errors; 16 timeouts.
- optimized tier uses 92/164 distinct cards across 15 archetypes (top cards: Bog Catalyst x30, Flame Dart x28, Pyre Surge x24, Spark x23).
- distinct cards per band x quality BOSS cell: all in range 59-130 (e.g. 61-70|optimized=91, 61-70|random=96, 61-70|naive=85).
- PROBE SIM (scripts/expand-validation-matchups.mjs + deck-battle-sim.test.ts) on the 2 thinnest cards at boss7: probe-counterdefwater vs boss_iron_golem WR=1.00 cushion=0.664; probe-attatkwater vs boss_iron_golem WR=1.00 cushion=0.435 — consistent with quality-matrix.json 61-70 optimized bossCushion=0.687.
impact_if_wrong: If the per-quality-tier depth gap were actually load-bearing for the headline, the flat-curve conclusion could be a coverage artifact: e.g. if optimized only ever reused a handful of S-tier cards, its high cushion would be a sampling illusion and a true mastery gradient could be hidden. My checks rule this out (cells pool 59-130 cards; thin cards still clear deep content). The real residual risk is NARROWER: ~45 cards have card-level verdicts resting on uncurated decks, so any DOWNSTREAM per-card nerf/buff decision (P4 re-derivation) for those cards is under-evidenced and could be wrong — but that does not move the curve headline.
recommended_action: Accept the flat-curve headline on coverage grounds — breadth is sufficient and probe sims confirm robustness. Do NOT let per-card verdicts for the 45 never-curated cards (and the 29 cards in <10 decks) feed P4 balance changes without targeted top-ups: generate >=2 curated decks per under-sampled card (especially the 45 with no naive/optimized appearance) before drawing card-level conclusions. Also: strengthen the analyzer's coverage flag from binary covered to a depth tier (e.g. covered/thin/curated) so thin-evidence cards are visibly flagged in card-appearance.json rather than silently reported as covered=true with bossWR=1.

detail: Every boss winRate/cushion cell in the headline matrix is dangerously thin and the "4 repeats" advertised in the brief add ZERO statistical power because the harness is fully deterministic. Verified facts: (1) 0 of 2045 matchups (0 of 327 boss matchups) have an intermediate winRate — every matchup is either 0.0 or 1.0, so the 4 repeats per matchup always agree. The effective sample of a cell is the DISTINCT-DECK count, not the ~40 "fights." (2) Boss-cell deck counts: 1-10 {random14,naive13,opt13}; 11-20 {random10,naive13,opt13}; 21-40 {random20,naive13,opt13}; 41-60 {random20,naive13,opt13}; 61-70 {random10,naive13,opt13,mixed7}. Granularity is one deck = 7.7-14.3 percentage points. (3) MOST boss cells test a SINGLE boss (1-10=doom_knight only; 11-20=iron_golem only; 61-70=boss_iron_golem only; the naive/optimized 21-40 and 41-60 cells each see only ONE boss). So the win/cushion of a cell is "how do ~10-13 specific decks do against ONE specific boss," not a robust band estimate. (4) Every non-1.0 boss winRate in the matrix is driven by EXACTLY ONE deck flipping to a total loss: the famous 61-70-random "0.90" = 9/10 decks win, 1 deck (rand-boss7-mage-0c, a chaotic floor deck) times out on all 4 repeats. Same single-deck-flip pattern for every 0.92/0.95 cell. Whether thinness THREATENS the flat-curve claim: it is double-edged, so I rate it NEUTRAL. It does NOT clearly threaten the claim — the flat result is so saturated (almost everything is 1.0 with high cushion across 443 decks and ~1700 normal matchups) that even with wide CIs the central tendency is unambiguously "easy." But the precise headline numbers are NOT trustworthy as point estimates, and the single-boss-per-cell design introduces a confound that makes the quality-tier comparison unsound: in band 41-60 the random cell averages desert_golem (0.86 cushion) AND infernal_dragon (0.62 cushion) while naive/optimized see ONLY desert_golem — so "random vs optimized" in that band isn't comparing the same boss. The random-vs-optimized cushion gap is statistically NOT resolvable in 4 of 5 bands (Welch t<1.96), and in band 21-40 random has HIGHER cushion than optimized (0.92 vs 0.72, t=3.49) — backwards from any mastery gradient, more consistent with boss-confound + tiny n than a real measurement.
evidence: tests/audit/val/analysis/quality-matrix.json (nBoss per cell: 61-70 random=10, naive=13, opt=13, mixed=7). Determinism: 0/327 boss matchups and 0/1718 normal matchups have 0<winRate<1; all boss matchups have repeats=4 yet always-unanimous outcomes. Headline cell 61-70 random, row-level: rand-boss7-warrior-0..3 + -0c + rand-boss7-mage-0..3 all winRate=1; rand-boss7-mage-0c winRate=0 (timeouts=4, deaths=0) — i.e. 9/10 win, Wilson95%CI=[0.60,0.98]. Every 1.00 cell with n=13 has Wilson lower bound ~0.77 (consistent with true WR as low as 77%). Single-boss confound: 41-60 random faced desert_golem (n10, WR0.90, cushion0.86) AND infernal_dragon (n10, WR1.00, cushion0.62), while 41-60 naive/optimized faced ONLY desert_golem (enemy-summary / per-row enemy field). Gen tooling scripts/gen-random-decks.mjs uses perCell≈4 per (class,stage) → ~8-10 random decks/stage; analyze-validation-results.mjs lines 126-149 build cells by distinct deck.id with no minimum-n guard. WORKFLOW-BRIEF.md lines 105-125 demand ">=6 repeats for boss claims" and "Run several enemies to avoid a single-matchup fluke" — both effectively unmet (repeats are inert; most cells = 1 boss).
impact_if_wrong: If the thinness were treated as adequate, two real risks: (a) the published per-cell point numbers (e.g. "final boss 0.90/0.83") would be cited with false precision — the true value for that cell spans [0.60,0.98] and rests on one deck's timeout; (b) the quality-tier comparison underpinning "deck quality barely matters" is partly an artifact of cells facing DIFFERENT bosses, so any future fix validated against this matrix could be tuned to the wrong target. Conversely, over-rotating on this concern could wrongly discredit the flat-curve conclusion, which the aggregate (near-total saturation across 443 decks) still supports directionally.
recommended_action: 1) Stop reporting per-cell boss numbers as point estimates; report them with the deck-count n and a Wilson interval (the matrix should emit nDecks-based CIs, not just means). 2) Treat the 4 repeats as decorative — either inject real RNG/seed variation per repeat (verify deck-battle-sim makeRun seeding) or drop repeats and instead 3-4x the DECK count per boss cell (target n>=30 decks/cell for a load-bearing winRate). 3) Make every (band x quality) cell face the SAME set of bosses so the quality comparison is apples-to-apples; today 41-60 and 21-40 random cells average a different boss mix than naive/optimized. 4) Re-derive the flat-curve claim from the per-deck saturation rate across the full library (which IS well-powered: ~1700 normal + 327 boss matchups) rather than from the sparse per-cell boss winRates. 5) Flag band 61-70 mixed (n=7) and the 11-20/61-70 random (n=10) cells as too thin to be load-bearing.

detail: Per-cell build quantity is genuinely THIN, but it does NOT overturn the flat-curve claim. The boss winRate/cushion in each (band x quality) cell is backed by very few decks: random n=14/10/20/20/10 and naive/optimized n=13 each across bands 1-10..61-70; the thinnest cell is 61-70 mixed at n=7. Worse, the effective sample is even smaller than nBoss suggests because the per-deck boss outcome is near-deterministic: of 327 boss matchups, 319 won 4/4 and 8 lost 0/4 with ZERO partial results. Within-deck variance is ~0, so the 4 repeats per deck add almost no statistical power — effective n = number of distinct DECKS (7-20), and each contributes a binary win/lose. Three further compounding issues: (1) the run used boss repeats=4, below the brief's own >=6 standard for boss claims (line 107) and below the expand-script default of 8; (2) each 'band' cell aggregates only one or two bosses (61-70 = boss_iron_golem only; 41-60 = desert_golem+infernal_dragon), so band cells are single-boss point estimates, not a true band; (3) every non-1.0 headline winrate rests on a SINGLE deck's loss — 61-70 random '0.90' is 9/10 (one chaotic-mage timeout), 41-60 random '0.95' is 19/20. With n=10 all-but-one wins the 95% CI on the true win rate is roughly [0.56, 0.997]; the n=7 mixed cell has a rule-of-three loss upper bound of ~0.43. So individual cell numbers should NOT be quoted to 2 decimals as if precise. HOWEVER, a robustness re-sim threatens nothing: regenerating random boss7 decks with a fresh seed (99999 vs 1234), n=26 (2.6x), and 8 repeats (2x) still yields meanWR 0.962 / cushion 0.617 with 25/26 decks beating the FINAL boss. The qualitative claim — even random builds clear the whole game with comfortable HP — reproduces under independent sampling. Thinness inflates the apparent precision of the matrix but does not manufacture the flat curve.
evidence: quality-matrix.json + my per-cell recount of results-v2.json: boss DECKS per cell = 1-10[r14/n13/o13], 11-20[r10/n13/o13], 21-40[r20/n13/o13], 41-60[r20/n13/o13], 61-70[r10/n13/o13/mixed7]; all boss matchups repeats=4 (327/327). Per-matchup boss winRate distribution across the whole run: {1: 319, 0: 8} — no 0.25/0.5/0.75, i.e. per-deck binary. boss-report.json: 61-70 boss_iron_golem worst case is rand-boss7-mage-0c winRate 0 via 4 timeouts (the single loss behind random '0.90'); 41-60 desert_golem single loss rand-boss5-warrior-1 (timeouts 4) behind random '0.95'. Each band maps to <=2 bosses (BAND_OF in analyze-validation-results.mjs; my recount: 61-70 bosses={boss_iron_golem}). Robustness sim (fresh, seed 99999, 26 random boss7 decks, boss repeats=8): boss_iron_golem meanWR=0.962, meanCushion=0.617, winRate distribution {8:25, 0:1}; warrior 12/13, mage 13/13 — still binary even at 8 reps, confirming within-deck variance ~0. Rule-of-three: n=10 all-win => loss upper bound ~0.30; n=7 => ~0.43; n=13 => ~0.23.
impact_if_wrong: If the thin cells WERE the only support, a single different random seed or a couple more unlucky decks could have flipped a cell from e.g. 1.00 to 0.75, making the curve look like it has a real mastery gradient (random struggling late) when it does not — or vice versa. Quoting n=7-20 single-boss point estimates to two decimals as 'random 0.90 / naive 0.92 / optimized 1.00' invites over-interpreting noise as a quality ordering; those three numbers are statistically indistinguishable at this n. The risk is using the matrix to claim a precise gradient (or precise flatness) that the sample cannot resolve.
recommended_action: Do not quote individual cell win rates to 2 decimals or infer a quality ordering from them at this n. For any cell used to make a pass/fail balance decision, raise to >=25-30 distinct decks per (band x quality x class) and split bands so each boss is reported separately (don't merge two bosses into one band). Since per-deck boss outcomes are near-deterministic, spend the budget on MORE DECKS, not more repeats (drop boss repeats to 4-6, that is fine). Re-run the high-bands (41-60, 61-70) random/naive cells with a second independent seed and report the pooled estimate plus a Wilson 95% CI per cell so reviewers see the uncertainty band. The flat-curve headline itself is safe to keep — it reproduced under fresh-seed, larger-n, higher-repeat re-sampling — but present it as 'random builds clear the final boss in ~96% of sampled decks (n=36 pooled, CI ...)' rather than as exact per-cell point values.

detail: Two separable questions. (A) Within-tier ARCHETYPE/ELEMENT/CLASS spread: the deck pool is genuinely diverse, NOT clustered. Element entropy is 0.95-1.00 in every band x quality cell; archetype entropy 0.88-0.93; all 11 card-archetypes appear in essentially every cell; class is a balanced ~50/50 warrior/mage (note: "class" is a synthetic resource-pool label invented by gen-random-decks.mjs — the game has no class field, only stamina vs mana + 8 elements at 55 cards each, so the only real "class" axis is 2-valued resource pools, but it is balanced). Random decks average 6-8 distinct elements / 6-8 distinct archetypes per deck (bigger grab-bags), optimized decks are focused (~5). So the matrix is NOT biased by all-one-archetype clustering — the surface "armor-dominant random" impression is a mode-of-distribution artifact, not real clustering. (B) A genuine BUILD x ENEMY coverage gap does exist: two of seven bosses — bog_witch (boss4, band 21-40) and infernal_dragon (boss6, band 41-60) — have ZERO naive and ZERO optimized decks in the 443-deck library; they are faced only by random and mixed. This makes the headline a non-apples-to-apples cross-tier comparison within a band: band 41-60 "optimized 1.00/0.78" is desert_golem ONLY while "random 0.95/0.73" averages desert_golem + the harder infernal_dragon; band 21-40 random/mixed cushions are inflated by the EASIEST boss (bog_witch, random cushion 0.97) that naive/optimized never see. I verified the gap is in the deck library itself (boss4 qualities {random:10, mixed:15}; boss6 {random:10, mixed:9}; no N/O), not merely an analyzer grouping issue.
evidence: quality-matrix.json + per-boss join of results-v2.json: doom_knight random/naive/opt 1.00/1.00/1.00; bog_witch random 1.00 cushion0.97, naive=NONE, opt=NONE; infernal_dragon random 1.00 cushion0.62, naive=NONE, opt=NONE; desert_golem random 0.90/naive 0.92/opt 1.00; boss_iron_golem random 0.90/naive 0.92/opt 1.00. Entropy script over decks-v2-full.json: elemEntropy 0.95-1.00, archEntropy 0.88-0.93, nArchTypes=11 in all 20 cells. Probe sim (retargeted optimized boss5 decks -> bog_witch & infernal_dragon, 8 boss reps): war-pure-physical bog_witch win=1.0 cushion0.80 / infernal_dragon win=1.0 cushion0.63; war-bleed-rage bog_witch win=1.0 cushion0.87 / infernal_dragon win=1.0 cushion0.77 — no hidden cliff. Note final-boss random cell is thin: band 61-70 random nDecks=10, nBoss=10.
impact_if_wrong: If the coverage gap were directionally hostile it could flip the curve read, but it is not: random wins >=0.90 on EVERY boss including the two hardest (infernal_dragon 0.62 cushion, boss_iron_golem 0.90 win), and optimized clears the two missing bosses cleanly in my probe. So the qualitative headline ("even bad/random builds clear the whole game; flat curve, no mastery gradient") survives per-boss. What IS unsafe is reading the band-level cross-tier cushion NUMBERS literally (e.g. concluding random is comfier than optimized in band 21-40 — that delta is partly the bog_witch easiest-boss confound, not a real quality inversion).
recommended_action: Author naive + optimized decks for boss4 (bog_witch) and boss6 (infernal_dragon) so all four quality tiers face the identical 7-boss set, then re-run the matrix; until then, present the boss matrix PER-BOSS (not band-averaged) so the differing enemy sets per tier cannot masquerade as a quality effect. Also thicken the band 61-70 random cell (currently only 10 decks / 10 boss games) before leaning on the 0.90 final-boss number. None of this changes the flat-curve conclusion, but it removes a confound a reviewer could exploit.

detail: Build diversity within each tier/band is healthy, not clustered, and does not bias the matrix. CLASS: the load-bearing `random` tier is exactly 50/50 warrior/mage in all 5 bands (classMaxFrac=0.50); naive/optimized are 61/39 mage (identical across bands because they are the same 13 archetype decks at two quality levels); mixed ranges 54-71% mage. No band x quality cell is single-class and none exceeds 71% one class. ARCHETYPE: mixed spans 24-37 distinct archetypes per band (max archetype share <=12%); naive/optimized cover the same 13 designed archetypes one-each per band (each ~8%); only `random` is "clustered" at 80-95% the random archetype — which is correct by design (random is undirected, not an archetype). ELEMENT: all 8 elements (agility/air/attack/counter/defense/earth/fire/water) appear in every cell; dominant-element max-frac stays 0.20-0.46 with Shannon entropy 1.8-2.9, and the random tier spans all 8. CRITICAL adversarial check: the 61/39 mage skew in the designed tiers does NOT move the headline — counterfactually re-weighting naive/optimized to 50/50 class shifts boss cushion by only -0.027 (naive) and +0.004 (optimized); for the load-bearing random tier the delta is 0.000 because it is already balanced. Within designed tiers all 13 archetypes contribute equally (10 boss matchups each) with cushion spread 0.476-1.000, so no single strong archetype is carrying any cell and even the weak slow/stun build (0.70 WR) is represented. The flat-curve conclusion cannot be attributed to archetype/class/element clustering.
evidence: Per band x quality (unique decks feeding each matrix cell): random 1-10 class={warrior 50%, mage 50%} classMaxFrac=0.50, domElement spread water 23%/air 18%/.../agility 5% (elemMaxFrac=0.23, H=2.86); naive/optimized every band classMaxFrac=0.62 (mage 62%) with 13 archetypes at archMaxFrac=0.08; mixed 1-10 archMaxFrac=0.12 across 37 archetypes. Global pooled: random n=120 {warrior 60, mage 60}; naive/optimized n=71 {mage 43, warrior 28}; mixed n=181 across 44 archetypes. Counterfactual (scripts/diversity-probe2.mjs): random warriorCush=0.772 mageCush=0.800 observedPooled=0.786 (50%war) -> 50/50=0.786 delta=0.000; naive warriorCush=0.651 mageCush=0.838 observedPooled=0.771 (35%war) -> 50/50=0.744 delta=-0.027; optimized delta=+0.004; mixed delta=+0.005. Designed-archetype boss perf (naive+optimized pooled, 10 matchups each): cushion 0.476 (burn-detonators) to 1.000 (defensive poison), WR all 1.00 except air/earth slow+stun=0.70 (range 0.524). SINGLE-CLASS/SINGLE-ARCH cell scan printed zero flags. Element universe = 8 elements, all present in random tier. Scripts: scripts/diversity-probe.mjs, scripts/diversity-probe2.mjs.
impact_if_wrong: If diversity were actually clustered (e.g. all-warrior or one dominant archetype per cell), the flat-curve headline could be a sampling artifact — a single overpowered archetype/class repeated across the grid would inflate winRate/cushion uniformly and falsely read as "no mastery gradient." My checks rule this out: composition is balanced and the class skew that does exist (naive/optimized 61/39 mage) would, if corrected, slightly LOWER naive's cushion rather than raise it, so it cannot be manufacturing the flatness.
recommended_action: No diversity fix needed; this dimension supports the headline. Two minor hardening notes for the report: (1) document that naive/optimized share the same 13-archetype skeleton (so their 61/39 mage skew is structural, not a sampling accident) and ideally rebalance those to 50/50 class for cleaner cross-quality comparison in future passes; (2) keep reporting per-archetype min/max cushion alongside cell means so a future regression where one archetype carries a cell would be visible. The flat-curve root-cause investigation should stay focused on hero-stat-vs-enemy scaling (other parameters), not deck composition.

detail: The three quality tiers do NOT separate the way the headline requires, and the defect is in the RANDOM tier's construction. gen-random-decks.mjs samples UNIFORMLY from a card pool that is 73% Tier-3 (120/164 cards) at every loop8+ stage, so "random" decks are 69% T3 (median 73%, p90 92% T3). A uniform draw from a T3-dominated pool just reproduces the strongest tier — the "bad build" tier is really "a random pile of mostly the best cards." Quantified separation: avg peakOPS random=21.16, naive=21.92, optimized=29.44 — random is INDISTINGUISHABLE from naive and only ~28% below optimized. The real sim confirms it: boss winRate random 0.973 > naive 0.954 (the supposedly-worse tier wins MORE), and boss cushion is INVERTED random 0.786 > naive 0.771 > optimized 0.686. The genuine floor (random-chaotic) is only 14 decks, boss-only, and still guaranteed >=2 damage cards. My controlled probe is the clincher: a deliberately-terrible warrior deck (lowest-OPS castable cards, zero finisher) finishes lizard_king at 0.09 cushion over 102s, whereas the gen-random "bad" deck cruises to 0.73 cushion in 24s — basically as comfy as optimized (0.68/12.7s). So the random tier behaves like a good/mid deck, not a floor. The N->O half of the design IS sound (same archetype/size pairs; naive lacks the engine, optimized front-loads auras + finisher suites; +34% OPS, boss win 1.00 vs 0.95), so optimized is genuinely better. The flaw is one-sided: the floor is inflated.
evidence: tier composition: random 69.2% T3 / naive 38.8% T3 / optimized 61.8% T3 (pool itself is 73.2% T3, so random ~= pool). peakOPS: random 21.16, naive 21.92, optimized 29.44 (LATE: 21.38/22.17/29.11). Real results-v2 joined by deck-id->quality: boss win random 0.973 / naive 0.954 / optimized 1.000; boss cushion 0.786 / 0.771 / 0.686 (inverted vs intent). Controlled floor-probe sim (boss3 lizard_king, SIM_MAX_MS=180000): TRUEFLOOR win 1.00 cush 0.09 ttk 102000ms; GENRAND win 1.00 cush 0.73 ttk 24400ms; OPT win 1.00 cush 0.68 ttk 12700ms (all genuine wins, well under the 180s cap). gen-random-decks.mjs line 87-93: pick(pool) with pool=classPool over tiers [1,2,3], no tier weighting; line 47 pick = arr[floor(rng*len)] uniform. Late random decks 38/58 are entirely T2+ (no chaff).
impact_if_wrong: The headline says "even RANDOM/bad builds clear the whole game at 0.90 win / 0.83 HP." Because the random tier is accidentally T3-heavy and good, the 0.83-HP figure is an artifact of mislabeled-strong decks, not evidence that bad play is safe. A properly-bad random tier would show the boss cushion collapsing (my true-floor hit 0.09), i.e. there IS a meaningful cushion/TTK gradient that the inflated random tier and win-rate saturation are masking. The qualitative claim "boss winRate is ~flat near 1.00" survives (even my true-floor deck won 100%), so the curve is genuinely too easy on the win/lose axis — but the specific 'random clears at 0.83 cushion' number, and any conclusion that 'deck quality barely matters,' is not trustworthy as stated. Acting on it (e.g. nerfing hero scaling to fix a curve that random allegedly trivializes) could overcorrect, since the real discrimination lives in cushion/TTK that this tiering hid.
recommended_action: Fix the random generator before re-deriving the headline: (1) weight random draws by tier-availability per stage instead of uniform-over-pool (e.g. early stages mostly T1/T2; cap T3 fraction to what a clueless drafter would actually own), or sample inversely to power so the floor is genuinely weak; (2) add a real low-floor cohort (no guaranteed finisher, allow off-resource/anti-synergy) and report it separately from the current chaotic-14. (3) Re-run the matrix and report the boss CUSHION/TTK gradient, not winRate, as the discrimination metric (winRate is saturated at 1.00 even for a deliberately-terrible deck, so it cannot distinguish tiers). (4) Re-state the headline using the corrected floor: keep 'boss winRate is flat/too-easy' (robust) but drop or re-measure 'random clears at 0.83 HP' and 'deck quality barely matters,' since true-floor cushion was 0.09 vs optimized 0.68.

detail:
The "random" (bad-build) tier is MISLABELED at deep bands, which is a test-design flaw that invalidates the headline's stated root cause. gen-random-decks.mjs samples uniformly from a class pool whose tier distribution is 120/164 (73%) T3 (T1=8, T2=36, T3=120). At boss stages it allows tiers [1,2,3] with deck sizes 13-15, so a "random" deep deck is overwhelmingly T3. Measured tier composition across boss5-7: RANDOM decks are 77% T3 / 19% T2 / 3% T1, which is actually MORE T3-heavy than OPTIMIZED decks (68% T3 / 20% T2 / 12% T1). Because the design charter makes every T3 a high-ceiling strong card ("too strong is fine"), a random PILE of T3 cards is not a bad build — it is a strong-card deck that merely lacks synergy/curve. So the build-quality axis is measuring synergy/SPEED, not raw power, and the claim "even RANDOM/bad builds clear the whole game" is an artifact of the generator, not evidence that deck quality is irrelevant.

HEAD-TO-HEAD (the requested test): 3 optimized vs 3 random warrior decks vs the final boss boss_iron_golem (boss7, enemy 611 HP / 15 dmg, 12 repeats each). Optimized: win 1.00 / cushion {0.688, 0.802, 1.00}, TTK {11.9s, 9.0s, 22.4s}. Random: win 1.00 / cushion {0.668, 0.933, 0.941}, TTK {41.0s, 37.0s, 30.9s}. Win rate is IDENTICAL (1.00 vs 1.00) and HP cushion is NOT better for optimized — two of three random decks finished with HIGHER HP than two of three optimized. The ONLY meaningful difference is time-to-kill: optimized is ~2-4x faster. So optimized does NOT outperform random by a meaningful survival/win margin; it just kills faster.

PROOF THAT A REAL GRADIENT EXISTS (and the curve is NOT truly flat): I authored two GENUINELY weak warrior decks for boss7 — T1-dominant with defensive dead weight and ~3 real attackers (the build the curve was supposed to model failing). Both crushed all normals (werewolf/baby_dragon/depths_horror: win 1.00, cushion 0.92-1.00) but FAILED the final boss: the T1-heavy deck timed out 12/12 (332 avg dmg, cannot break 611 HP) and the defense-stall deck died 12/12. Optimized reference: win 1.00 / cushion 0.688. So a true bad build loses the final boss 0/12 while optimized wins 100% — a mastery gradient DOES exist at bosses; the test just never produces a genuinely-weak deck at depth because "random" is a T3 pile.
evidence: Card pool tier dist (cards-index.json): T1=8, T2=36, T3=120 of 164; warrior pool T1=5/T2=23/T3=98 of 126. Deck tier composition (decks-v2-full.json): rand-boss7-warrior-0/1/2 = T3{11,12,11}, T2{2,2,3}, T1{1,1,1}; optimized war-pure-physical/bleed-rage/armor-finisher = T3{9,8,8}, T2{3,5,6}, T1{3,2,1}. Aggregate boss5-7: random 77%T3/19%T2/3%T1, optimized 68%T3/20%T2/12%T1. Head-to-head boss7 (_audB_boss7_results.json, 12 reps): optimized win 1.00 cushion{0.688,0.802,1.00} ttk{11900,9000,22400}ms; random win 1.00 cushion{0.668,0.933,0.941} ttk{41000,37000,30900}ms. True-weak boss7 (_audB_trueweak_results.json, 12 reps): trueweak-t1heavy win 0.00 timeouts 12 dmg332; trueweak-defensestall win 0.00 deaths 12 dmg322; opt-pure-physical win 1.00 cushion0.688 — yet both weak decks beat every normal at 1.00 win / 0.92-1.00 cushion. Mechanism: gen-random-decks.mjs lines 56-66 (boss stages tiers:[1,2,3], size 13-15) + line 88 uniform pick over a 73%-T3 pool. No deck carries stats/runXP/relics (all 443 decks), so quality differs ONLY by card list — confirming the artifact is purely the tier sampling, not stat differences.
impact_if_wrong: If accepted uncritically, the headline drives a misdiagnosed fix: "nerf hero stat scaling so deck quality matters." But the sim shows hero scaling is fine — a genuinely weak deck (low damage output) already loses the final boss 0/12. Nerfing hero stats would punish all builds and break the early/mid game (true-weak decks already clear normals only with comfortable cushions). The actual issue is that the random generator overstates the bad-build tier's power at depth (T3 pile), so the test cannot SEE the existing gradient. A stat nerf based on this would over-correct and likely make average/mid builds non-viable while leaving the real lever (the curve/synergy/closing-speed difference) untouched.
recommended_action: Fix the random-deck generator before trusting the flat-curve conclusion: cap T3 share for the "random/bad" tier at deep bands (e.g. weight sampling so deep random decks are majority T1/T2 with at most a few T3, OR add a "genuinely weak" tier that mirrors the trueweak decks I authored). Re-run the quality matrix with corrected random decks; I predict deep-band random boss win rate will drop well below 1.00, restoring a visible mastery gradient. Separately, since optimized already differs from random by TTK (~2-4x) but NOT by win/cushion, report the curve verdict in terms of TIME-TO-KILL and closing reliability, not just win rate. Keep the head-to-head artifacts (tests/audit/val/_audB_boss7_results.json, _audB_trueweak_results.json) as the worked example. Do NOT recommend a hero-stat-scaling nerf on the current evidence.

detail: Boss sampling is uneven and, for 2 of 7 bosses, missing entire quality tiers — but closing the gap does NOT reverse the flat-curve headline. (1) Per-boss quality breakdown (joining results-v2 to decks-v2-full by deck id): doom_knight 14R/13N/13O/38M, iron_golem 10R/13N/13O/23M, lizard_king 10R/13N/13O/22M, desert_golem 10R/13N/13O/9M, boss_iron_golem 10R/13N/13O/7M — adequate, all 3 tiers present. But bog_witch (n=25) = 10 random + 15 mixed and infernal_dragon (n=19) = 10 random + 9 mixed: ZERO naive and ZERO optimized decks. This is baked into the deck library at source — STAGE x QUALITY counts show boss4 and boss6 have no naive/optimized decks authored, while every other boss stage has 13+13. (2) The quality-matrix MASKS this: BAND_OF lumps boss4 into band 21-40 (shared with lizard_king) and boss6 into band 41-60 (shared with desert_golem), so the 'optimized'/'naive' cells for those bands are fed 100% by lizard_king / desert_golem. The headline's 'band 41-60: optimized 1.00/0.78 | naive 0.92/0.88' is desert_golem only; infernal_dragon's naive/optimized boss performance was never simulated yet the band reads as if all bosses were covered. (3) These two are also simply the thinnest boss samples (n=19, n=25 vs 43-78 elsewhere). (4) I closed the gap with a confirming sim and the verdict held — so this is an evidence-presentation flaw, not a verdict-flipping one.
evidence: Source deck library STAGE x QUALITY (decks-v2-full.json): boss4 {random:10, mixed:15} — NO naive/optimized; boss6 {random:10, mixed:9} — NO naive/optimized. Contrast boss1 {14R,13N,13O,38M}, boss5/boss7 {10R,13N,13O,...}. Band-quality boss contributors (matchup counts): '21-40|optimized'={lizard_king:13} (bog_witch:0); '41-60|optimized'={desert_golem:13} (infernal_dragon:0); '41-60|naive'={desert_golem:13} (infernal_dragon:0). CONFIRMING SIM: re-tagged real optimized/naive decklists (from boss3/boss5/boss7) onto boss4 and boss6 at IDENTICAL scaling (bog_witch scaledHP=471/dmg8 loopMult1.3; infernal_dragon scaledHP=575/dmg11 loopMult1.5 — exact match to results-v2), ran expand-validation-matchups.mjs + deck-battle-sim.test.ts: bog_witch naive 6 matchups win=1.000 cush=0.841, optimized 10 matchups win=1.000 cush=0.860; infernal_dragon naive 6 win=1.000 cush=0.573, optimized 10 win=1.000 cush=0.685. All 1.00 win, cushions in line with the random/mixed numbers already reported (bog_witch 0.919, infernal_dragon 0.61) — gap closure reinforces, not reverses, the flat curve.
impact_if_wrong: If a reader trusts the quality-matrix band cells at face value, they will believe all 7 bosses were stress-tested across random/naive/optimized when 2 of them (the two thinnest, n=19 and n=25) were only ever faced by random+mixed decks. Any claim like 'optimized comfortably clears band 41-60' is unsupported for infernal_dragon specifically. Had infernal_dragon behaved differently under optimized play (e.g., a degenerate combo trivializing or, conversely, a wall), the band aggregate would have hidden it. My probe shows it did not, so the headline is not endangered — but the coverage assertion implied by the matrix is false and should not be repeated as 'all bosses x all tiers'.
recommended_action: 1) Author/inject 13 naive + 13 optimized decks for stages boss4 (bog_witch) and boss6 (infernal_dragon) and re-run, so every boss has true 3-tier coverage (the probe decks I wrote to tests/audit/val/probe-decks.json + probe-results.json already demonstrate the method and outcome). 2) Make analyze-validation-results.mjs emit a per-boss x quality coverage table (and warn when a boss is missing a tier) instead of only band-level rollups, so missing tiers cannot be masked by band-mates. 3) In the report, qualify the late-band 'optimized/naive' boss numbers as boss5/boss7-only and footnote that bog_witch/infernal_dragon optimized/naive were confirmed separately (1.00 win, cushion 0.86/0.685). 4) Raise n for the two thin bosses (currently 19/25) to match peers.

detail: Boss quality-tier coverage is severely uneven. Verified from raw results-v2.json joined to decks-v2-full.json (each boss matchup = 4 repeats): bog_witch (boss4, n=25 matchups) and infernal_dragon (boss6, n=19, the thinnest boss) were faced by ZERO naive and ZERO optimized decks — their entire sample is 10 random + (15 / 9) mixed. Root cause confirmed in the deck library: curated naive/optimized tiers (13 each) exist only at stages boss1/boss2/boss3/boss5/boss7; loop15, loop25, boss4, boss6 have naive:0 optimized:0. Consequently the headline matrix's late-band quality cells each rest on a SINGLE boss (band 41-60 naive/opt = desert_golem only; band 61-70 = boss_iron_golem only), and 2 of the 3 deep bosses have no curated-tier data at all. I empirically tested the gap by cloning the 52 boss5/boss7 naive+optimized decks onto boss4/boss6 (identical loop scaling — bog_witch scaledHP 471 matched the original) and running the sim. The gap does NOT hide a difficulty wall: naive/opt cushions land in the same low band as random (bog_witch ~0.83-0.89 vs random 0.97; infernal_dragon ~0.65 vs random 0.62) — no positive mastery gradient, which actually reinforces the flat-curve claim. HOWEVER it concealed a real datum: against fire-affinity infernal_dragon the optimized mage-pure-burn deck DIED 4/4 (winRate 0), dropping optimized to 0.923 — yet that same deck wins every other boss. So infernal_dragon's boss-report verdict (meanWinRate 1.0, nLossesMatchups 0, fracDecksAnyLoss 0) is a sampling artifact, not a true clean pass. Net: the macro flat-curve headline is not overturned (neutral), but the late-game quality axis it leans on is single-boss-deep and one boss's clean-pass verdict is false.
evidence: Per-boss tier coverage (matchups): doom_knight r14/n13/o13/m38; iron_golem r10/n13/o13/m23; lizard_king r10/n13/o13/m22; bog_witch r10/n0/o0/m15; desert_golem r10/n13/o13/m9; infernal_dragon r10/n0/o0/m9; boss_iron_golem r10/n13/o13/m7. Stage x quality library counts confirm boss4=naive:0/opt:0, boss6=naive:0/opt:0. Band->naive/opt single-boss mapping: 41-60 naive={desert_golem:13} opt={desert_golem:13}; 61-70 naive={boss_iron_golem:13} opt={boss_iron_golem:13}. PROBE SIM (cloned boss5/7 naive+opt decks onto boss4/boss6, 4 reps): bog_witch naive 1.000/cush0.894 opt 1.000/cush0.827 (0 losers); infernal_dragon naive 1.000/cush0.653 opt 0.923/cush0.663 with 2 losses (mage-pure-burn-boss6-O wr=0 deaths=4). Same optimized pure-burn deck in original run beats doom_knight(cush0.13), iron_golem(0.23), lizard_king(0.23), desert_golem(0.42), boss_iron_golem(0.13) — only dies to fire-affinity infernal_dragon (affinity=fire, multi_hit x3 + enrage). Cushion-by-tier (no gradient): infernal_dragon random(10)0.62 vs naive/opt absent originally; with probe naive 0.65/opt 0.66 ~ random.
impact_if_wrong: If left unaddressed, the boss-report's per-boss verdicts for bog_witch and infernal_dragon (and the 41-60/61-70 quality-matrix cells) are not robust: infernal_dragon is reported as a flawless 100%/0-loss pass when an optimized burn deck deterministically dies to it, masking a fire-affinity matchup weakness. Any per-boss balance decision (e.g. 'infernal_dragon needs no change') drawn from the current sample would be wrong. The macro flat-curve conclusion survives, but its late-game quality dimension is effectively n=1 boss per cell, so a reviewer should not treat the optimized-tier late-game numbers as well-sampled.
recommended_action: Add the missing curated naive + optimized decks for loop15, loop25, boss4 (bog_witch) and boss6 (infernal_dragon) — at minimum the 13-archetype panel used at boss1/2/3/5/7 — and re-run expand-validation-matchups + analyze. Re-state infernal_dragon's boss verdict as 'optimized burn deck loses (fire-affinity)', not a clean 100% pass. When reporting the late bands, footnote that naive/optimized cells rest on a single boss each (desert_golem for 41-60, boss_iron_golem for 61-70). Keep the flat-curve headline but qualify it: confirmed for random/mixed across all 7 bosses and for naive/opt on the 5 covered bosses + my probe, with no positive mastery gradient observed.

detail: The headline's suspected root cause — hero stat scaling from runXP outpacing enemy scaling, so deck quality barely matters at depth — is CONFIRMED, and the progression modeling that produces it is REALISTIC (not a test artifact). (a) Achievability: a loop-walk with the config's actual density (basicTileCombatChance 0.18, eliteChance 0.15, loopGrowth schedule) yields ~77-93% of the model's runXP (560/1150/1700/2300/2900/3600/4400); at a denser-but-still-plausible 0.30 combat chance the player EXCEEDS the model by 1.2-1.5x. So the granted runXP is in-ballpark to slightly generous — the big late hero stats are legitimate for someone who actually reached that loop. (b) Self-consistency: the validation does NOT thread a single surviving deck through bosses 1-7; each rand-bossN deck is an independent list handed runXP=stage.runXP by fiat (expand-validation-matchups.mjs L78), with no requirement it beat earlier bosses. This is a simplification, BUT it is data-supported because the boss-report shows random warrior decks genuinely win bosses 1-6 at ~1.0 winrate with healthy cushions, so reaching late game is plausible for them. (c) Driver quantified and empirically isolated: from boss1->boss7 hero maxHP scales 1.00->1.61 (157->253 HP) and STR 1.00->2.50, while boss threat scales only 1.00->1.30 because the normalMult of 1.6 (percentPerBossKill 0.10) is halved by the isBoss half-rate in scaleEnemyForLoop to 1.3. Bosses get RELATIVELY easier with depth. The half-rate ALSO suppresses absolute boss damage (final boss deals just 15 dmg/hit into a 253-HP hero).
evidence: DECISIVE SWAP TEST (identical rand-boss7-warrior-0 deck vs boss_iron_golem, 8 repeats each): runXP=4400 (granted) -> winRate 1.00, cushion 0.668, 0 deaths; SAME deck at runXP=0 (no progression) -> winRate 0.00, 8/8 deaths. Stripping the runXP bonus flips a comfortable win into a guaranteed death, proving progression stats are the dominant driver, not deck quality. Third arm (runXP=4400 but boss at FULL normalMult 1.6, override baseHP 752/dmg 19) still wins but cushion drops 0.67->0.36, showing the boss half-rate inflates cushion materially. | Scaled boss stats in results-v2.json: doom_knight HP380/dmg6, boss_iron_golem HP611/dmg15 (611 = 470 base x 1.3 half-rate, NOT 752 = base x 1.6). | Hero re-derivation (XPSystem getLevel base50/growth1.15 + HeroStatsResolver getInRunLevelBonus +6 maxHP/level, +1 VIT & +1 STR per 2 levels, VIT*5->maxHP): boss1 L7 -> 157 maxHP/STR4; boss7 L18 -> 253 maxHP/STR10. | XP achievability walk: at combatChance 0.18 achievable/model ratio = 0.77/0.81/0.87/0.90/0.93/0.93/0.91 across boss1-7; at 0.30 = 1.19..1.43. | expand-validation-matchups.mjs L78 `const runXP = d.runXP ?? stage.runXP` and decks-v2-full.json decks carry no runXP, so XP is injected purely from stage label.
impact_if_wrong: If runXP were unrealistic the flat curve would be an artifact; achievability analysis shows it is realistic, so the headline holds. The boss half-rate amplifies (not creates) the effect.
recommended_action: Treat the flat-curve conclusion as TRUSTWORTHY. To FIX the underlying problem (not the test): (1) reduce the boss half-rate or raise percentPerBossKill so boss threat keeps pace with hero maxHP/offense growth — currently bosses scale 1.3x while hero scales 1.6x HP / 2.5x STR over the same span; (2) flatten the in-run maxHP curve (+6/level + VIT*5) which is the single biggest survivability lever. For test HARDENING (optional, to remove the by-fiat-runXP simplification): add one threaded-run scenario where a single random deck must actually clear bosses 1->7 with carried-forward runXP earned from real combats, to confirm the independent-deck + granted-XP shortcut matches an end-to-end run.

detail:
The headline's ROOT-CAUSE mechanism (hero stat scaling outpaces enemy scaling, so deck quality barely matters at depth) is REAL and I independently confirmed it. But the headline FRAMING ("even RANDOM/bad builds clear the whole game") rests partly on a test-design artifact: the validation never simulates a continuous run. Each matchup is an INDEPENDENT per-tier snapshot with runXP injected directly from the stage model (expand-validation-matchups.mjs:78 `const runXP = d.runXP ?? stage.runXP`). A boss7 result asserts "a level-18 hero with this fresh-seeded random deck beats boss_iron_golem" — it NEVER establishes that THAT deck could have beaten bosses 1-6 to legitimately reach level 18. The `rand-boss7-*` decks have ZERO overlap with the `rand-boss1-*` decks (0/14 shared), so there is no chain of evidence that one random build survives the whole game; there are 7 disjoint snapshots, each handed a leveled hero for free.

(a) Achievability: my conservative re-derivation of earnable runXP (loopLen schedule [3,2,2,1,1], 18% combat-tile chance, 85% normal@10 / 15% elite@30, +80/boss) gives ~431 XP@L10, ~932@L20, ~1480@L30, ~2075@L40, ~2693@L50, ~3335@L60, ~4000@L70. The stage model INJECTS 560/1150/1700/2300/2900/3600/4400 — roughly 15-30% HIGHER than earnable. Since hero power is a monotonic function of runXP, this optimism inflates hero stats at every boss tier, biasing toward the flat-curve result.

(c) Driver quantified: loops 1->70 heroMaxHP 100->253 (+153%), heroStr 1->10 (+900%); enemy normalMult only 1.0->1.6 (+60%) and boss EFFECTIVE mult only 1.0->1.3 (+30%, due to the half-rate plus bossMultiplier=1.0). My isolation sim (identical 10-card deck vs boss_iron_golem) proves leveling is the offense driver: leveled hero dealt 573 damage vs only 20 unleveled — a ~28x swing from runXP alone. So hero leveling IS the dominant mechanism, exactly as the headline suspects.
evidence: expand-validation-matchups.mjs:78 injects stage.runXP per-matchup with no progression gate; deck-battle-sim.test.ts makeRun seeds hero.runXP=m.runXP and createCombatState clamps to resolved maxHP (base + getInRunLevelBonus + VIT*5). HeroStatsResolver.getInRunLevelBonus: maxHP=level*6, vit=floor(level/2), offense=floor(level/2). XPSystem.getLevel(4400)=18 -> warrior maxHP=253, str=10. DifficultyScaler.scaleEnemyForLoop: boss path loopMult=1+(nMult-1)*0.5 then *bossMultiplier(=1.0). Recorded scaled bosses: doom_knight HP380/dmg6@mult1.0; boss_iron_golem HP611/dmg15@mult1.6. boss-report.json: random decks win every tier (doom_knight 1.00 cushion0.62, iron_golem 1.00/0.83, lizard_king 1.00/0.87, bog_witch 1.00/0.97, desert_golem 0.90/0.86, infernal_dragon 1.00/0.62, boss_iron_golem 0.90/0.83). Deck-overlap probe: boss1 vs boss7 random deck-id overlap = 0. Isolation sim probe-results-x.json: b7-leveled dmgDealt=573, b7-NOlevel dmgDealt=20 (same deck, same boss).
impact_if_wrong: If reviewers take "even random clears the WHOLE game" literally and balance around it, they may over-buff enemies or gut hero leveling to make a CONTINUOUS random run fail — but the test only proved 7 disjoint, hero-leveling-subsidized snapshots win, not that one random deck survives loops 1-70. Conversely the genuine, verified problem (hero power +153% HP / +900% STR vs enemy +60% / boss +30%) would be UNDER-fixed if dismissed as pure artifact. The injected runXP being ~15-30% above earnable also means the live game is marginally less flat than the sim suggests; tuning to the sim numbers would slightly over-correct.
recommended_action: 1) Add a CONTINUOUS-RUN self-consistency sim: thread a single deck through loops 1->70, accumulating runXP from actual combats earned (not injected), and only test boss N+1 if the SAME deck cleared boss N — this is the test that would actually substantiate (or refute) "random clears the whole game." 2) Replace injected stage.runXP with a simulated-accrual model (or cap it at my derived earnable curve ~431/932/1480/2075/2693/3335/4000) so hero power is not over-credited. 3) Fix the real driver: rebalance percentPerBossKill (0.10) upward and/or remove the boss half-rate so enemy threat growth (currently +30-60%) tracks hero growth (+153% HP/+900% STR); or flatten getInRunLevelBonus offense/HP slopes. 4) Re-run the matrix after (1)-(2) before grading against the Moderate standard.

detail: The harness is correct on every axis in scope, so the flat-curve numbers are real engine output, not a measurement artifact. (1) REAL ENGINE: deck-battle-sim.test.ts imports and drives the production CombatEngine tick loop with createCombatState, scaleEnemyForLoop, resolveHeroStats — no stubs/mocks. Win detection (enemyHP<=0 && heroHP>0) and isComplete are the real engine's. (2) FULL-HP SEEDING: the currentHP:1_000_000 sentinel is clamped by createCombatState (Math.min(currentHP, resolved.maxHP)) so the hero enters at the FULL resolved maxHP, not base. An instrumented probe over all 8 canonical profiles returned fullHP:true every time (heroHP===heroMaxHP===resolvedMaxHP). (3) HERO STATS FROM runXP: independently recomputing getLevel + getInRunLevelBonus + VIT*5 reproduces the resolver and the brief exactly — warrior offense->STR, mage offense->INT, mage INT stays low (3 at loop-10). (4) ENEMY/BOSS SCALING: all 45 distinct (enemy,mult,isBoss) tuples in results-v2 match scaleEnemyForLoop with ZERO mismatches; boss half-rate (loopMult = 1+(mult-1)*0.5, bossMultiplier=1.0) is applied exactly once — the expander deliberately passes normalMult and lets isBoss halve it internally (no double-application). (5) ZERO errored matchups (0/2045); win/death/timeout accounting sums to repeats in every row. SECONDARY OBSERVATION (does not threaten headline): the engine is effectively deterministic for these decks — all 2045 winRates are exactly 0.0 or 1.0 (no fractional), and a 6-repeat boss probe produced bit-identical HP cushion, so the `repeats` field adds no RNG averaging; confidence rests on breadth across decks/enemies, not repeat variance.
evidence: 3-boss spot-check (recorded vs formula): doom_knight mult 1.0 -> HP 380=floor(380*1.0); iron_golem mult 1.1 -> loopMult 1.05 -> HP 420=floor(400*1.05); infernal_dragon mult 1.5 -> loopMult 1.25 -> HP 575=floor(460*1.25). 45/45 enemy tuples matched, 0 mismatches. Seed probe (tests/audit/deck-battle-sim.test.ts:228,261 logic): warrior runXP 520 -> resolvedMaxHP 151, combatHeroHP 151, fullHP true, STR 4 VIT 3 DEX 1; mage runXP 520 -> resolvedMaxHP 121, combatHeroHP 121, INT 3. results-v2.json meta count 2045, 0 errored, 0 accounting mismatches. doom_knight band-1-10 reproduced from 78 raw boss rows: aggregate winRate 0.987 (~0.99), win-weighted cushion 0.591 (~0.59) — matches headline; only 1/78 rows had a death (cushion min 0.008) proving the harness DOES record losses, all 78 used scaledHP 380. Determinism: 0/2045 fractional winRates; 6-repeat probe cushion 0.099/0.099 (warrior) and 0.339/0.339 (mage) bit-identical.
impact_if_wrong: If seeding or scaling were wrong it would directly fabricate the flat curve — e.g. seeding base maxHP would UNDERSTATE survival (~40% of resolved HP) and make the curve look HARDER, while double-counting the boss half-rate would over-scale bosses and also make them look harder; both verified absent, so neither inflates the easy-clear conclusion. The only live risk would be the reverse (harness somehow over-crediting HP/under-scaling enemies), and the explicit formula spot-checks plus the recorded loss rows (deaths/timeouts present, cushions spanning 0.008..1.0) rule that out. The determinism observation only weakens per-matchup confidence intervals, not the level of the win/cushion numbers, which reproduce exactly from raw rows.
recommended_action: Accept the harness as correct for the flat-curve headline. Two low-priority follow-ups for the synthesizer, neither blocking: (1) note in the report that `repeats` provides no RNG averaging (engine is deterministic for these decks) so robustness comes from deck/enemy breadth, not repeat counts — drop or de-emphasize the 'use >=6 repeats for boss claims' guidance. (2) Reconcile a DATA (not harness) discrepancy: WORKFLOW-BRIEF lists doom_knight at 9 dmg but enemies.json has 6 dmg; the harness faithfully used 6, so any 'first boss too easy' claim should cite the live 6-dmg value.

detail: Re-confirmed the harness is correct end-to-end and measures the real CombatEngine, so the flat-curve numbers it produced are trustworthy at the measurement layer. (1) Full-HP seeding works: a live probe through the real createCombatState showed heroHP === heroMaxHP in all 6 sampled profiles (seededFull:true); a leveled warrior@4400XP starts 253/253, not base 100 — the prior "leveled hero starts ~66%" bug is genuinely fixed. impact_if_wrong: If the harness understated hero HP (the old base-HP seeding bug) the flat-curve headline would be WRONG in the dangerous direction — heroes would look more fragile than they are, masking the over-survivability the headline claims.
