detail: winRate is a near-constant, NON-discriminating metric in this matrix: 97.6% of boss cells and 99.7% of normal cells are exactly 1.0, and across all 2045 matchups winRate is strictly binary (0 or 1 — zero values between). It saturates almost everywhere, so the headline's "boss winRate 1.00" rows carry no information about quality. CUSHION is the only metric the matrix varies on — but cushion is INVERTED vs build quality: in EVERY loop band, optimized decks show LOWER boss cushion than random/naive (band 21-40: random 0.916 > naive 0.796 > optimized 0.717). The matrix does NOT distinguish "comfortable" from "fast-but-risky": it systematically mislabels the strongest builds as least comfortable, because optimized decks RACE — TTK is the metric that cleanly orders quality (in every band optimized kills ~2x faster than random, e.g. 16208ms vs 40645ms in band 21-40), taking proportionally more HP in a shorter high-tempo exchange while random tanks slowly to high end-HP. Two further structural quirks: (1) The sim is effectively deterministic at the win/loss boundary. The engine uses UNSEEDED Math.random (SharedRNG falls back to Math.random; the sim never calls setActiveRNG), so the "repeats to average over RNG" the brief mandates is real noise — yet I ran the lowest-cushion wins 20x each: a 0.008-cushion (0.8% HP) win went 20/20 while its sibling loss went 20/20 (all deaths). Outcomes are bimodal — a deck either crushes with stable margin or loses unanimously; there is almost no "sometimes wins" zone, so winRate has no intermediate states by construction and the repeats are wasted compute. (2) winRate conflates two failure modes the brief treats as distinct: 8 of 14 losses are TIMEOUTS (deck too slow to close in 3 min), not deaths — both collapse to winRate=0. (3) Deepest issue: the sim resets the hero to FULL max HP every encounter (createCombatState clamps heroHP to resolved maxHP from the 1,000,000 sentinel), so cushion-on-win measures single-encounter survivability against a rested hero and CANNOT observe cumulative run attrition — the very mechanism a "Moderate" curve uses to make bad decks bleed out over a loop chain. Net: cushion-on-win alone is the WRONG mastery metric; a valid score must weight TTK (clean quality ordering) and reliability, and ideally model attrition. HOWEVER — none of these quirks manufacture the flat curve. Random-quality decks win 72/74 boss matchups with MEDIAN cushion 0.863 (only 5 squeakers <0.3 HP), so they genuinely dominate at full HP rather than barely surviving. The metric quirks make the matrix harder to read and would UNDERSTATE a mastery gradient if one existed — but the raw signal (random builds clear the whole game with large margin) holds, so the flat-curve reading is safe.
evidence: results-v2.json: 2045 matchups, winRate values = {0:14, 1:2031}, 0 strictly between (fully binary); 0 matchups with split outcomes (0<wins<repeats) despite repeats 2-4. Boss winRate frac==1 = 0.976; normal = 0.997. Cushion-by-quality (boss wins) per band (cushion|ttkMs): 1-10 random 0.620|42293, naive 0.597|29592, opt 0.551|22923; 11-20 random 0.827|28150, opt 0.696|20715; 21-40 random 0.916|40645, naive 0.796|23431, opt 0.717|16208; 41-60 opt 0.779|17800 vs naive 0.875|29592; 61-70 random 0.829|39433, opt 0.687|18523. TTK monotonically orders quality in all 5 bands; cushion inverted/noisy. Determinism stress (_mv1-stress.json, 20 reps, unseeded Math.random): mage-pure-burn-boss1-1 vs doom_knight 20/20 win @0.008; mage-pure-burn-boss1-2 0/20 (20 deaths); poison-burn 20/20 @0.024; slow-stun-control-O 20/20 @0.016. SharedRNG.ts L19-21: rand()=activeRNG?random():Math.random(); sim never sets activeRNG. CombatState.ts L228: heroHP=Math.min(run.hero.currentHP,resolved.maxHP), currentHP=1_000_000 sentinel -> full HP every encounter, no attrition. Losses: 8 timeouts vs 6 deaths among 14 winRate=0. Random-quality boss: 72/74 wins, win-cushion median 0.863, only 5 wins <0.3 HP.
recommended_action: Stop treating winRate as a discriminating cell metric — it is saturated/binary; report it only as a pass/fail gate. Promote a composite mastery score that LEADS with TTK (cleanly orders quality) plus reliability, e.g. report TTK-by-quality alongside cushion and split timeouts out of winRate so "too slow" is not conflated with "died." Re-label cushion as single-encounter survivability, not "comfort," and never read optimized's lower cushion as difficulty (it is racing). To make the curve claim airtight, add an attrition-chain mode (carry heroHP across a sequence of encounters per loop instead of resetting to 1,000,000) and re-run random-vs-optimized; that is the only way the per-encounter sim can validate a "bad decks bleed out by mid-game" Moderate target. None of this changes the bottom line: random decks win 72/74 bosses at median 0.863 cushion, so the flat-curve headline stands on the raw data even though its presentation metric (winRate) is the wrong one.
impact_if_wrong: If reviewers trust winRate as the primary cell value, they read the all-1.00 boss rows as proof of a flat curve when winRate simply has no dynamic range — the real conclusion rests entirely on cushion, itself a flawed (inverted, attrition-blind, single-encounter) proxy. Anyone refuting the flat-curve claim via optimized's lower cushion as "optimized is pressured" would be citing a tempo artifact, not difficulty. Because the sim never models cumulative attrition, true in-run difficulty for a bad deck could be somewhat HARDER than the matrix shows (chained HP loss) — but it cannot be easier, so the flat-curve direction is safe. The live danger is using cushion deltas (dominated by tempo, not survivability) to drive per-card or per-boss tuning.

detail:
The sim is FULLY DETERMINISTIC, not stochastic: all 2045 matchups have winRate exactly 0 or 1 (zero matchups with 0<wins<repeats). `deck-battle-sim.test.ts` never calls setActiveRNG, so rand() falls back to UNSEEDED Math.random() (SharedRNG.ts:19-21), yet the RNG paths (enemy ±20% damage, stun/AI chances) never flip the binary outcome and the deck plays in fixed order. I re-ran the thinnest-cushion matchup (cushion=0.008) at 16 repeats: 16/16 wins with IDENTICAL ttk/damage/HP. So `repeats` (2/4/8) adds no statistical info; the test comment 'averaged over RNG' is false in practice and winRate is a pure 1-bit signal.

Consequently winRate is saturated and barely discriminating (boss: 319/327=97.6% at 1.0, only 2 distinct values, sd=0.154; normal 99.7%). CUSHION is the real signal (sd=0.236, 78 distinct values) — the prompt's suspicion is correct.

But cushion-on-win is a CONFOUNDED mastery metric and is INVERTED vs deck quality. Optimized cushion < naive in all 5 bands, and optimized < RANDOM in 4/5 bands. The racing hypothesis holds (optimized kills bosses ~19.2s vs random ~38.6s) but the inversion persists even controlling for boss identity (doom_knight: random 0.620, naive 0.597, optimized 0.551) AND for class (random/mage 0.800, naive/mage 0.838, optimized/mage 0.672). Global TTK-vs-cushion r=0.010, so cushion does NOT cleanly encode 'fast=safe' — it conflates 'barely survived' with 'won fast by design' and actively ranks BETTER decks as LESS comfortable. The matrix therefore cannot distinguish 'comfortable' from 'fast-but-risky' and has no reliability/TTK weighting.
evidence: determinism: 0/2045 matchups with 0<wins<repeats; winRate distribution {0:14, 1:2031}. Probe re-run (mage-pure-burn boss1 vs doom_knight, 16 reps): wins=16/16, cushion=0.008, ttk=30700, dmg=383 every repeat (zero variance). Saturation: boss winRate mean 0.976 sd 0.154 (2 distinct vals); boss cushion mean 0.748 sd 0.236 (78 distinct). Inversion (quality-matrix.json bossCushion): 1-10 rand .62/naive .597/opt .551; 11-20 .827/.816/.696; 21-40 .916/.796/.717; 41-60 .731/.875/.779; 61-70 .829/.786/.687. Racing (boss wins): random ttk 38649ms cushion .786 | naive 28056 .771 | optimized 19234 .686. Class-controlled cushion: random/mage .800, naive/mage .838, optimized/mage .672. Pearson r(TTK,cushion)=0.010 over 319 boss wins.
impact_if_wrong: The flat-curve headline leans on TWO metrics with opposite reliability. winRate is trustworthy but coarse and saturated — it genuinely shows random clearing the final boss (9/10; 100% through band 41-60), supporting 'no winRate mastery gradient.' But the CUSHION half is misleading: because it ranks random/naive ABOVE optimized, any reading of cushion as 'comfort/mastery' is inverted. This OVERSTATES flatness in one direction (random looks falsely comfortable) and HIDES the real gradient that does exist (optimized's 2x-faster TTK + clean 100% final-boss winRate). If a reviewer cites 'optimized has lower cushion than random, so quality doesn't matter,' that conclusion is a metric artifact, not a balance fact. Determinism also means every reported cell is a single sample — no confidence intervals; the 8 borderline losses are knife-edge and could be one-off.
recommended_action: 1) Stop reporting `repeats` as if averaging RNG — either seed the RNG via setActiveRNG per repeat (to get genuine variance and meaningful winRates) or set repeats=1 and label the sim deterministic; current repeats waste compute and imply false confidence. 2) Do NOT use cushion-on-win as the mastery axis without a TTK/HP-flux companion. Replace or augment with a duration-normalized survival metric, e.g. 'HP lost per boss attack window' or min-HP-fraction-during-fight, so racing (fast, low cushion) is not penalized as fragility. 3) Add a reliability dimension (min cushion / fraction of wins below a danger threshold — 9 boss wins are <0.20 cushion) so 'comfortable' and 'fast-but-risky' separate. 4) Re-grade the flat-curve claim on winRate + min-HP only, treating cushion-on-win as descriptive, not evaluative. The winRate-based flat-curve finding survives; the cushion-based 'optimized not more comfortable' sub-claim should be retracted or reframed as a racing artifact.

detail: Confirmed: a very large fraction of matchups end before the deck completes one pass, so back-of-deck cards and the "15-card deck works" claim are largely UNTESTED for composition — the test fixes an arbitrary authored ORDER instead. This is a genuine measurement-validity flaw. BUT it does NOT threaten the flat-curve headline; it points the other way. (1) Reproduced headline numbers exactly from quality-matrix.json. (2) Cycling fraction by band (avgReshuffles<1 = deck never wrapped): band 1-10 ~30%, bands 11-20/21-40/41-60/61-70 = 77/79/81/77%. Against NORMAL enemies it is 85-93% in bands 11-70; deckSize grows 8->14 while avgCardsPlayed stays flat ~8, so the untouched tail grows with depth. For optimized late decks (boss5-7), 60% of matchups play FEWER THAN HALF the deck. (3) Order-reversal experiment (boss7, deckSize 15, 6 repeats): reversing the SAME cards flipped mage-pure-burn boss from winRate 1.0 -> 0.0; warrior pure-physical boss cushion fell 0.688->0.213; bleed-rage boss cushion 0.802->0.549; vs normals winRate stayed 1.0 but cushion swung +/-0.13 and only 3-10 of 15 cards ever played. So boss outcomes hinge on a variable (card order) the harness fixes arbitrarily, and normal-enemy composition past the front ~8 cards is never measured. (4) Crucially, the artifact does not hide random-deck weakness: random/bad decks kill SLOWLY, so they cycle MORE, not less — boss matchups: random avg cardsPlayed 24-38 with reshuffles 1.35-16.4 and 0-10% no-wrap, vs optimized 10-14 cards, ~0.2-0.85 reshuffles, 69-77% no-wrap. Random decks are stress-tested through the whole deck plus multiple reshuffles and STILL win at deathRate~=0.000 across every band against both normals and most bosses. The flat curve is therefore a real survivability result, not a fast-kill mirage. The one interpretive caveat: the cushion-gradient inversion the headline cites (optimized cushion < random, e.g. band 21-40 opt 0.717 vs rand 0.916; band 61-70 opt 0.687 vs rand 0.829) is partly an artifact — optimized decks burst-kill in 5-12 cards at lower HP%, while slow random decks grind at high HP%, so the cushion metric conflates kill-speed with safety and exaggerates the 'no mastery gradient' read.
evidence: tests/audit/val/results-v2.json (2045 matchups; fields avgCardsPlayed, avgReshuffles, deckSize, deaths). No-wrap (avgReshuffles<1) by band: 1-10=29.9%, 11-20=77.1%, 21-40=78.7%, 41-60=80.5%, 61-70=77.3%. NORMAL-only: 11-20..61-70 = 85/88/93/91% no-wrap, avgCardsPlayed ~7.7-8 vs avgDeck 11.4->14.2. BOSS-only cycling by quality (band 21-40): random cardsPlayed 24.4/resh 1.35/10% no-wrap; optimized 10.6/0.23/77% no-wrap. NORMAL deathRate=0.000 for random in EVERY band incl. 61-70. Order-reversal probe (probe-order.json -> probe-order-results.json, 6 repeats, boss7 stage, scaleEnemyForLoop normalMult=1.6): mage-pure-burn-boss7-O vs boss_iron_golem ORIG wr=1 cush=0.126 -> REVERSED wr=0; war-pure-physical-boss7-O vs boss ORIG cush=0.688 cp=9 -> REVERSED cush=0.213 cp=21 resh=1; vs normals cp=3-10 of 15, resh=0. Engine CombatEngine.ts plays FIXED ORDER via deckPointer (no shuffle); cardsPlayed counts only executed cards (unaffordable/stunned advance pointer without counting), reshuffles counts full wraps — so reshuffles<1 is the precise 'deck never cycled' signal. quality-matrix.json reproduces headline boss numbers (1-10 opt 1/0.551, 61-70 opt 1/0.687).
impact_if_wrong: If my read is wrong and cycling DID hide random-deck failure, the flat curve would be a fast-kill artifact and the whole 'no mastery gradient' headline would be invalid. I tested this directly and falsified it: random decks cycle MORE and still win at deathRate 0, so the headline's core 'everything clears' claim is robust to the cycling concern. The residual risk is interpretive, not directional: (a) the optimized-deck back half (~40-65% of late decks) and 'full 15-card deck works' are genuinely unvalidated — a balance change that nerfs a back-of-deck card would not register in this matrix; (b) boss winRate/cushion are sensitive to authored card ORDER (a reversal flipped a win to a loss), so single-ordering boss numbers carry an unquantified order-variance band and per-deck boss results should not be over-trusted to 2 decimals.
recommended_action: Keep the flat-curve headline — this concern reinforces rather than undermines it. To harden the matrix: (1) Report avgCardsPlayed/deckSize and a 'deck-coverage %' alongside winRate so readers know which results exercised the whole deck; flag any cell where median coverage < 50% as 'composition under-measured.' (2) For deck-quality claims, either (a) randomize/rotate starting deck position across repeats, or (b) lean on the swap-test (scripts/swap-test.mjs) which deliberately tests an EARLY slot, since that is the only zone reliably reached against normals. (3) Treat per-boss winRate/cushion as order-dependent: run each boss deck under 2-3 orderings (forward/reverse/shuffled) and report the spread, since a reversal flipped a 1.0->0.0 boss win. (4) De-emphasize cushion as a quality discriminator vs normals/fast kills — it conflates kill-speed with safety; prefer deathRate + reshuffle count for survivability and avgTtk for power. None of these change the conclusion that random/bad decks clear the game.

detail: A large and band-dependent share of matchups end before the deck completes one pass, and crucially this affects NORMAL enemies (the bulk of the matrix and the "random clears everything / high cushion" narrative). For normals: band 1-10 = 31% never cycle, but bands 11-20 / 21-40 / 41-60 / 61-70 = 84% / 88% / 92% / 91% complete ZERO deck passes (avgReshuffles==0). Mean cards-played stays flat at ~8 while mean deck size grows from ~8 to ~14, so on average ~4-6 back-of-deck cards never resolve at depth. Because CombatEngine plays the deck in FIXED ORDER (no shuffle; gen-random-decks just appends picks), only the front ~8 slots are ever exercised vs normals at depth. Consequence: deck composition past the front half is structurally UNMEASURED, so the headline's "deck quality barely matters at depth" is confounded — quality differences living in the back of the deck are invisible to the matrix, which biases the result toward "quality doesn't matter." The analyzer that builds the quality-matrix never references avgCardsPlayed/reshuffles/deckSize, so it aggregates win/cushion blindly across fights that never tested most of the deck. Bosses are less affected (35-54% no-cycle, ~16-22 cards played), so the boss winRate/cushion numbers themselves are more trustworthy than the normal-enemy cushion numbers — but the "even random/bad builds clear the whole game" claim rests heavily on normals.
evidence: results-v2.json (2045 matchups), NORMALS only, %no-cycle (avgReshuffles==0): 1-10=31.2%, 11-20=83.8%, 21-40=87.9%, 41-60=91.7%, 61-70=90.7%; meanDeck vs meanCardsPlayed: 8.1/12.5, 11.4/7.8, 12.8/7.9, 13.6/7.7, 14.2/8.0 (≈4-6 untested back cards at depth). BOSSES only: %no-cycle 7.7/32.2/48.2/42.2/34.9, cards played ~16-22 (less affected). DECISIVE SWAP PROBE (loop50/runXP2900, 14-card warrior, 6 repeats): 'front-strong/back-strong' vs 'front-strong/back-JUNK (t1-counter/t2-counter-counter, 0 OPS)' produced byte-identical results vs all 5 normals — e.g. vs werewolf both = win 1.0, hpOnWin 0.962, cards 3, resh 0, ttk 4300, dmg 236; vs fire_elemental both = win 1.0, hp 0.949, cards 2, resh 0, ttk 3000, dmg 176 (normals scaled to only 141-189 HP at loop50). The boss probe (desert_golem 540HP) played 6 of 14 cards, back 8 untested, also identical between the two decks. analyze-validation-results.mjs: 0 references to avgCardsPlayed/cardsPlayed/reshuffle/deckSize — matrix is blind to cycling. CombatEngine.advanceDeckPointer marches the pointer sequentially and only counts reshuffles on wrap; cardsPlayed counts executed (not skipped) cards.
impact_if_wrong: If left unaddressed, the matrix systematically under-measures build-quality variance at depth: any deck whose differentiating power sits past slot ~8 is graded identically to a junk-tailed deck vs normals. This inflates confidence in "the curve is flat / quality doesn't matter," when the truth could be "the front of any deck trivializes gently-scaled normals, but back-half composition was never tested." The flat-curve conclusion for NORMAL clears (and the 15-card-deck-works claim) is therefore partly an artifact of fast kills, not a measured property of full decks. Note the direction is mixed: it does NOT rescue the curve (front cards really do over-clear normals, consistent with the leveling-outpaces-scaling root cause), but it makes the specific 'deck quality barely matters' inference unsafe and overstated.
recommended_action: 1) Add cycling diagnostics to the analyzer: report %matchups with avgReshuffles==0 and mean (deckSize - avgCardsPlayed) per band/quality, and flag any cell where >50% of fights never cycled as 'deck-undertested.' 2) Re-grade the quality matrix using ONLY matchups that completed >=1 full pass (or weight by fraction of deck played) to see if a mastery gradient emerges once back cards actually fire. 3) For depth bands, either (a) test against higher-HP normals / multi-enemy waves so fights last >=1 deck pass, or (b) explicitly randomize/rotate deck order across repeats so every slot gets front-line exposure — current fixed-order means a random deck's measured power == its random front-8 only. 4) Re-run the front-strong/back-junk swap probe across naive/optimized library decks (not just my synthetic burst deck) to quantify how many real matrix decks are front-loaded enough to be cycling-blind. Until (1)-(2) are done, qualify the headline: 'deck quality barely matters' is established only for the front half of decks vs normals; back-half and full-deck quality are unmeasured at depth.

evidence: Zero partial boss wins across all cells. Naive losses are three of 65, the slow stun control deck. Cushion rises with depth so the first boss is most pressured. A frozen level seven hero dies at late bosses while the scaled hero wins. The late random deck is a fifteen card tier three deck.
recommended_action: Lead with cushion and fraction wiped instead of winRate. Exclude broken decks. Encode explicit cushion thresholds per criterion. Grade per boss instead of folded bands. Hold a fixed bad build across bands. Add an attrition variant.
detail: Moderate criteria are not instrumented as gates. Boss outcomes are bimodal so winRate is near useless and dips are a few dead decks. Cushion is the only moving signal and confirms a flat inverted curve. Conclusion corroborated, not threatened.
impact_if_wrong: Graders may treat dead deck dips as a real gradient or miss the binary clear or wipe outcome, so nerfs could be mis sized.

detail:
The Moderate acceptance TARGETS are stated reasonably in prose (WORKFLOW-BRIEF: random wins to ~loop10-15 then FAILS; naive finishes but pressured late; optimized comfortable; doom_knight should be easy). But the quality x loop-band MATRIX that grades them measures a materially DIFFERENT question than the criteria state, in three compounding ways.

(1) STAGE-LOCKED HERO XP severs the criterion from its meaning. The Moderate criterion for 'random' is a single-run TRAJECTORY claim: a bad-build player should FAIL BY MID-GAME and the run ends. But the matrix gives every deck its STAGE's full runXP regardless of quality (0/443 decks override runXP/stats/relics — verified). So a 'random' deck at boss7 is handed runXP=4400 => level 18 (~253 maxHP), identical hero power to the optimized boss7 deck. The matrix therefore asks the counterfactual 'if a random deck somehow arrives at loop70 with endgame hero stats, can it win?' — which is far easier than, and not the same as, the stated criterion. This single instrumentation choice produces the 'even RANDOM clears the whole game' headline.

(2) The GRADING METRIC (winRate + HP cushion) is the one least able to detect a mastery gradient. Boss damage is a flat 3.7%-5.9% of hero maxHP per swing across ALL stages (loop10 doom_knight 6 dmg vs 157 HP = 3.8%; loop70 boss_iron_golem 15 dmg vs 253 HP = 5.9%), so even a slow kill ends with a big cushion. TTK clearly separates quality (random boss7 41000ms vs optimized 11900ms) but the matrix headline doesn't grade on TTK / damage-race margin, so 'pressured late' vs 'comfortable' is invisible to the chosen metric.

(3) The band->stage->boss mapping is lossy/unrepresentative. Bands lump bosses with different multipliers (21-40 = boss3@1.2 + boss4@1.3; 41-60 = boss5@1.4 + boss6@1.5). Worse, naive/optimized cover only ONE boss per band (boss4 and boss6 are NEVER tested for naive/optimized — verified from deck distribution), so the published 'band 41-60 optimized 1.00/0.78' is desert_golem alone, not the band.

There is also NO instrument anywhere in analyze-validation-results.mjs for the 'fails by mid-game' / attrition / trajectory criterion — only isolated per-stage checkpoints + the doom_knight easy-check.

These flaws do NOT reverse the flat-curve conclusion (the hero/enemy scaling mismatch is real and confirmed below), so I am NOT calling threatens_headline. But the matrix as built CANNOT validly grade the Moderate criteria as written and materially OVERSTATES the random-deck endgame result — hence 'concern' / 'neutral' rather than 'supports'.
evidence:
PROBE (tests/audit/val/probe-bands2.json, 8 boss-repeats, boss_iron_golem @loopMult 1.6) — random boss7 deck held fixed, only hero runXP varied:
  rand boss7 @ XP4400 (L18, MATRIX value): winRate 1.00, cushion 0.67, deaths 0, ttk 41000ms
  rand boss7 @ XP900  (L9, stalled bad-build reality): winRate 1.00, cushion 0.21
  rand boss7 @ XP560  (L7, loop-10 level): winRate 0.00, deaths 8/8  <-- random deck FAILS
  optimized boss7 @ XP4400 (control): winRate 1.00, cushion 0.69, ttk 11900ms
=> the random deck's endgame survival is almost entirely a function of the granted hero XP, not the deck; the deck-quality signal lives in TTK (41000 vs 11900), which the headline metric ignores.

SCALING MISMATCH (recorded enemyScaled* from results-v2.json vs XPSystem/HeroStatsResolver computation): boss HP 380->611 (x1.6) and dmg 6->15 (x2.5) from loop10->loop70, while hero goes L7->L18 (maxHP 157->253, +11 levels). Boss dmg-as-%-of-hero-HP stays flat 3.8%/5.4%/5.4%/3.7%/5.1%/4.5%/5.9% across boss1..boss7.

DECK DISTRIBUTION (decks-v2-full.json): naive/optimized = 13 decks each at boss1/boss2/boss3/boss5/boss7 + only 2 each at loop2/5/8; ZERO at loop15/loop25/boss4/boss6. random/mixed do span all stages. 0/443 decks override runXP, stats, or relics.

BAND_OF map (analyze-validation-results.mjs L112-118): {loop2,loop5,loop8,boss1}->1-10; {loop15,boss2}->11-20; {loop25,boss3,boss4}->21-40; {boss5,boss6}->41-60; {boss7}->61-70. No gating/attrition/trajectory metric exists in the analyzer (grep confirmed).

Per-stage random-deck winRate (results-v2.json): only loss outside endgame is loop2 (4 normal losses, 1 deck winRate 0 — soft-lock); boss1..boss7 all winRate ~1.0 (boss5/boss7 each have a single 0.90 from one repeat).
recommended_action:
Refine the grading so it measures the criteria as written:
(1) DECOUPLE deck quality from hero XP for the gradient test: re-run the matrix with hero runXP held at a FIXED reference (e.g. the loop-10 level, runXP~560) across all deep stages, OR add a 'realistic-trajectory' run where bad decks carry the XP a stalled player would actually have. The probe shows this flips the random endgame result from win 1.00/cushion .67 to 0.00 (8/8 deaths) — that is the number the 'random fails by mid-game' criterion is asking for.
(2) GRADE ON TTK / damage-race margin, not just winRate+cushion. Add a per-cell median avgTtkMs and a 'boss-win-margin' = (heroHP cushion AND ttk vs boss cd budget). 'pressured late' should be defined as cushion below a threshold OR ttk above a threshold — currently neither is checked.
(3) FIX the band mapping: report PER-BOSS (one cell per boss stage) rather than lumping boss3+boss4 and boss5+boss6; and ADD boss4/boss6 naive+optimized decks so the 41-60 / 21-40 cells aren't single-boss stand-ins. Re-label the headline bands by the actual boss tested.
(4) ADD an explicit gating instrument: simulate a run as a sequence (each stage's outcome conditions the next, with XP earned only on wins) so the 'random fails by mid-game' / 'naive pressured late' criteria can be graded as trajectories, not isolated checkpoints.
Keep the independently-confirmed root cause (hero scaling outpaces enemy scaling: boss dmg flat at ~4-6% of hero HP all game) as the real balance finding.
impact_if_wrong: If the matrix is used as-is to grade Moderate, it will (a) report PASS/FAIL on the random row for the wrong reason — it actually grades 'does an endgame-leveled hero with a bad deck win', not 'does a bad-build run survive to endgame', overstating random viability and masking that random genuinely fails when not gifted endgame XP; (b) be blind to the naive-'pressured-late' vs optimized-'comfortable' distinction because the cushion metric saturates and TTK is uninstrumented, so any fix aimed at restoring a mastery gradient cannot be measured as succeeding; (c) mislabel single-boss cells (desert_golem, lizard_king) as whole-band verdicts and never test boss4/boss6 at quality tiers, so a balance regression on the untested bosses would pass silently. The flat-curve HEADLINE itself stays true (scaling mismatch is real), but every downstream tuning decision graded against these bands risks being declared done when the criteria are not actually met.

detail: The flat-curve / no-mastery headline SURVIVES at its core (deep game is genuinely flat) but its EARLY-BAND evidence is materially OVERSTATED by two test-design biases. (1) runXP inflation: stage-model.json assigns runXP that is ~1.7-1.9x a realistic combat-by-combat accumulation in bands 1-10/11-20 and ~1.1-1.4x deep, over-leveling the hero by +1 to +2 levels at every stage (e.g. boss1 model runXP=560/L7 vs realistic ~431/L5). Leveling grants both maxHP and the offensive axis (STR/INT scales card damage), so over-leveling simultaneously pads cushion AND speeds kills, flipping marginal losses into wins. I RAN the sim to confirm: a real library random deck (rand-boss1-warrior-3) vs doom_knight WINS 5/5 at model L7 (cushion 0.338) but DIES 5/5 at realistic L5 and below — a hard loss converted to a win purely by over-assigned XP. The survivor (rand-boss1-warrior-1) decays from 0.459 cushion (L7) -> 0.393 (L5) -> 0.195 (L3) -> 0.01 (L0): a latent mastery/level gradient that inflated runXP flattens. (2) Survivorship bias: cushion is avgHeroHpPctOnWin (wins only), so marginal decks that die drop OUT of the cushion mean instead of pulling it down — the reported doom_knight 0.59 mean cushion hides a bimodal spread where 24/78 matchups (31%) win with <0.45 cushion and several mages win on 0.008-0.165 HP slivers (knife-edge at the INFLATED level; they would flip to deaths at realistic level). So 'even RANDOM/bad builds clear the FIRST boss with healthy cushion' is overstated. HOWEVER, the deep-game claim is clean: enemy scaling is applied correctly (boss7 loopMult=1.6, scaled HP 611 / dmg 15 verified in results-v2, NOT the double-halved 1.3 the script warns against), and dropping a deep random deck 2 levels barely moves it (still 0.77-0.95 cushion clears). The final boss hitting for 15 vs a ~250-HP hero is a genuine design problem, not a test artifact. Also a deck-quality-leakage note: gen-random-decks allows T3 (3-element crafted) cards in the 'random' tier from boss1 onward, so 'random' decks are stronger than a truly clueless player's deck — further inflating the early floor.
evidence: runXP vs realistic (combatChance 0.18, loopLen 15-40, 13 XP/combat): loop10 model 560(L7) vs real 431(L5) =1.30x; loop5 336(L5) vs 175(L3) =1.92x; loop8 480(L6) vs 281(L4) =1.71x; loop20 1150(L10) vs 932(L9) =1.23x. SIM (tests/audit/redteam-xp-probe-results.json): rand-boss1-warrior-3 vs doom_knight: model560/L7 win=1.0 cushion=0.338; real431/L5 win=0.0 deaths=5; xp200 win=0.0 deaths=5; xp0 win=0.0 deaths=5. rand-boss1-warrior-1: win stays 1.0 but cushion 0.459->0.393->0.195->0.01 across L7/L5/L3/L0. Survivorship: of 78 doom_knight matchups, 24 win with <0.45 cushion incl mage-pure-burn-boss1-1=0.008, mage-slow-stun-control-boss1-O=0.016, mage-poison-burn-boss1-2=0.024, rand-boss1-mage-5=0.157 — all at inflated L7. Deep-game robustness (tests/audit/redteam-deep-probe-results.json): rand-boss7-warrior-0 vs boss_iron_golem at model L18 cushion 0.846 vs L16 0.775 (still clears). Enemy scaling verified correct in results-v2.json: boss7 loopMult=1.6 enemyScaledHP=611 enemyScaledDamage=15 (boss_iron_golem raw HP470/dmg12). Quality leakage: scripts/gen-random-decks.mjs STAGE_CFG boss1.tiers=[1,2,3] -> rand-boss1-warrior-1 is 8/9 T3 cards.
impact_if_wrong: If acted on naively, the team could 'fix' the difficulty curve by nerfing hero HP/leveling or buffing all enemies, when in fact the EARLY-band over-difficulty masking is a test-config bug (runXP too generous + win-only cushion), not a balance fact. Conversely, if the early-band inflation is ignored, the report will under-state how punishing the first boss already is for off-meta mage builds (some win on <2% HP, and at realistic level they DIE) — i.e. the game may have a real early mage-cliff that the inflated test hides. The deep-game flatness conclusion is safe to act on; the early-band 'random always survives comfortably' conclusion is not.
recommended_action: 1) Re-run bands 1-10 and 11-20 with runXP set to the realistic combat-accumulation curve (loop10~431, loop5~175, loop8~281) instead of stage-model values, and re-derive the quality matrix — expect early cushions to drop and some random/off-meta mage decks to start losing the first boss, revealing the latent gradient. 2) Report cushion as avgHeroHpPctOverall (losses=0) or report a win-rate-weighted cushion alongside the win-only number to kill survivorship bias; flag the bimodal doom_knight distribution (31% sub-0.45). 3) Tighten gen-random-decks so the 'random' tier reflects un-crafted availability (cap T3 share early) to remove deck-quality leakage into the floor tier. 4) KEEP the deep-game flat-curve finding — it is robust to a +/-2 level swing and to correctly-applied enemy scaling; the root cause (boss damage scaling 1.6x at loop70 -> only 15 dmg vs ~250 HP hero) is a genuine design issue worth a separate enemy-scaling fix.

detail:
The headline's ROOT CAUSE (enemy scaling badly outpaced by hero growth) is real and I confirmed it. But its strong framing — "the curve is FLAT, there is NO mastery gradient, even RANDOM/bad builds clear the whole game (0.90 win / 0.83 HP)" — is OVERSTATED and partly an artifact of three independent test-design issues that all bias the curve flatter:

(1) DECK-QUALITY LEAKAGE in the "random" tier. gen-random-decks samples uniformly from tiers [1,2,3] at boss stages, but the catalog is 120/164 (73%) T3. So "random" boss7 decks are ~12-of-15 T3 cards (verified: rand-boss7-warrior-1 = 1×T1/2×T2/12×T3). T3 cards are the strongest in the game and require 3-element crafting — a clueless/unlucky loop-70 player would NOT own 12 of them. The "random" tier is really "randomly-composed but tier-rich," which props up the floor. When I built a REALISTIC weak deck (T1/T2 only) and ran it vs the final boss at current scaling, it barely scraped by at 0.115 cushion (vs 0.93 for the tier-rich random), and at a realistic lower level it DIED 8/8. A "misbuilt" low-damage deck TIMED OUT 0/8. And a pure-T1 deck LOSES to a basic early normal (mush) 0/8 — so the floor is NOT flat; weak builds do fail.

(2) METRIC ARTIFACT in "cushion." The headline reads high HP cushion as "no mastery gradient," but cushion rewards armor-stacking, not skill. In the real boss7 data, random=0.90WR/0.83cushion/39.4s-ttk vs optimized=1.00WR/0.69cushion/18.5s-ttk. Optimized decks have LOWER cushion because they're aggro (race to kill, take hits); random decks end high because they're stuffed with defensive T3s. Win rate (0.90 vs 1.00) does show a gradient; cushion inverts it. So "0.83 HP for random = comfortable = flat" misreads a defense-stacking artifact.

(3) The root cause IS confirmed though: I cranked enemy scaling in-sim and decks only START dying at 3-4x the boss base (the game ships ~1.30x at loop70). At 3.0x the optimized aggro warrior DIED 8/8 while the tanky random survived — proving deck composition matters once enemies are scaled to a Moderate target, and that the flatness for competent decks is driven by weak scaling, not by runXP (dropping runXP L18→L12 barely moved cushion 0.93→0.87; the weak-deck win/loss flip on level shows level matters only at the floor).
evidence:
Scaling math (DifficultyScaler + stage-model): boss mult passed = stage.normalMult (max 1.6 at loop70); boss half-rate = 1+(1.6-1)*0.5=1.30, ×bossMultiplier(1.0)=1.30. enemy-summary confirms boss_iron_golem base 470HP/12dmg → scaled only 611HP/15dmg (1.30x/1.25x) at the FINAL boss. Normals at loop70 only 1.6x.
Hero growth (computed): warrior maxHP loop2→loop70 = 117→253 (2.16x), plus STR/VIT/DEX growth and free armor cards — out-scales enemies.
My sim (random boss7 warrior vs boss_iron_golem, 8 reps each): @1.6x(611HP)→WR1.0/cush0.93; @3.0x(940HP)→WR1.0/cush0.42; @4.0x(1175HP)→WR1.0/cush0.03. Optimized aggro warrior: @1.6x→WR1.0/cush0.69; @3.0x→WR0/8 deaths; @4.0x→WR0/8 deaths. runXP 4400→1700 on random: cush 0.93→0.87, still WR1.0.
My sim (realistic-weak decks): T1/T2 warrior vs boss7 @cur → WR1.0 but cush 0.115; same deck @runXP900(L9) → 0/8 DEATHS; misbuilt low-dmg → 0/8 TIMEOUTS; pure-T1 vs mush early → 0/8 DEATHS.
Real results-v2 boss7 by quality: random 0.90WR/0.83cush/39.4s; naive 0.92/0.79/29.8s; optimized 1.00/0.69/18.5s (cushion inverts vs win-rate/ttk). Deck library: random=120, naive=71, optimized=71, mixed=181; quality-matrix headline rows correctly exclude 'mixed' so v1 pollution is NOT in the cited numbers.
impact_if_wrong: If accepted uncritically, the team may conclude the only fix needed is "flatten hero leveling / steepen enemy scaling" and expect that to restore a mastery gradient. That fix is necessary (enemy scaling at 1.30x for the final boss is clearly too weak) but the validation will keep reporting "flat" even after a real gradient exists, because (a) the "random" tier isn't actually a bad-build floor — it's a tier-rich deck, so the floor will stay artificially high, and (b) the headline grades on cushion, which rewards tanky decks and will mask the gradient. Conversely, the strong claim "even bad builds clear the whole game" is false as stated (realistic weak/misbuilt decks die or time out), so over-buffing enemies based on it could make genuinely-weak players unable to clear early content (a pure-T1 deck already loses to a basic normal).
recommended_action: Qualify the headline before acting: (1) Re-grade the random tier with a REALISTIC bad-build generator — cap T3 share to what a struggling player plausibly owns (e.g. weight tier draws by craftability, not catalog count), or add an explicit "low-tier floor" tier; rerun and report that tier's boss win/cushion separately. (2) Stop using HP cushion alone as the mastery signal — report win-rate AND time-to-kill AND a deaths/timeouts breakdown by quality; the cushion inversion (random 0.83 > optimized 0.69) is a metric artifact, not flatness. (3) Keep the confirmed root-cause finding (enemy scaling far too weak: final boss 1.30x, decks only stress at 3-4x) — this is the real, robust takeaway. (4) When re-validating any scaling fix, also re-run the realistic-weak and misbuilt decks I used here as the true floor, and watch early-game (pure-T1 already fails vs mush) so an enemy buff doesn't wall genuine beginners.
